nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater rivers, oxbow lakes, peat swamps, and flooded forest margins; typically near the surface in heavily shaded, tannin-stained, low-pH water with overhanging riparian vegetation

decorPreferences: large open swimming lanes near the surface
minimal tall décor to avoid impeding top-zone movement
driftwood and large rounded rocks as visual breaks
dim or subdued lighting with floating plants (Ceratopteris, Amazon frogbit) to reduce stress
secure tight-fitting lid mandatory — powerful jumper
gentle filtration with low surface turbulence

diet.notes: Apex surface predator. Wild diet consists of insects, frogs, small fish, birds, and small mammals that fall to the water surface. In captivity accepts large pellets formulated for arowanas, large sticks/carnivore pellets, whole feeder fish, large crickets, mealworms, earthworms, prawns, and cut fish fillet. Live prey triggers strong feeding responses but reliance on feeder fish carries disease risk; transitioning to prepared foods early (juvenile stage) is strongly recommended. Feed 1–2 times daily for juveniles; every other day for adults. Overfeeding causes fatty liver disease. Refuses small or floating flake foods.

goodWith: large robust bottom-dwellers kept well below the surface (large clown loaches, large Synodontis catfish, large plecos, large oscars if tank is sufficiently large)
large stingrays in purpose-built 2000+ L systems
large Bichirs in very large tanks
avoidWith: other Scleropages / arowanas (intense conspecific aggression; group keeping requires 4000+ L and expert management)
any fish small enough to be swallowed
nippy species (will attack arowana fins and eyes)
fish that compete for surface swimming zone

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Scleropages formosus; verify current availability before use.

summary: The Asian Arowana is a majestic, ancient predatory fish reaching 90 cm, prized in the aquarium trade for its metallic scales and cultural significance. CITES Appendix I — import banned in the USA; legal trade requires microchip certification. Needs a 1500 L+ tank and expert-level care.
careNotes: The Asian Arowana (Scleropages formosus) is one of the most demanding fish in the freshwater hobby, requiring enormous tank footprints (minimum 300 cm long × 90 cm wide), exceptional filtration, and meticulous water quality. Its elongated, torpedo-shaped body and powerful muscles make it an explosive jumper — a heavy, lockable lid is non-negotiable. Tank water should replicate the soft, slightly acidic blackwater of its native Southeast Asian peat-swamp habitat: pH 6.0–7.0, gH 1–10, temperature 24–30°C; tannin supplementation via driftwood or Indian almond leaves is beneficial. Perform 20–30% weekly water changes with dechlorinated, appropriately conditioned water. The species is intensely territorial and should typically be kept alone or with large, robust bottom-dwelling species that occupy different zones. Juvenile arowanas are especially skittish and prone to 'drop eye' (drooping eye syndrome) believed to be caused by habitually staring downward at bottom tankmates or light reflections — keeping décor minimal and using overhead lighting can help. Avoid any décor or equipment near the water surface that could injure a jumping fish. This species has exceptional longevity (10–20+ years) and may outlive inadequate setups; prospective owners must plan for decades of large-tank commitment. The trade in Asian Arowanas is strictly regulated under CITES Appendix I (listed since 1975); legal commercial trade occurs only from certified aquaculture farms (primarily in Malaysia, Indonesia, and Singapore) with individual fish microchipped, DNA-documented, and accompanied by CITES export permits. Import and sale in the United States is federally prohibited under the Endangered Species Act. Prospective owners outside the USA should verify import/ownership legality in their jurisdiction before purchase.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder: after spawning, the male carries the fertilised eggs — and subsequently the fry — in his buccal cavity for 6–8 weeks until the yolk sac is absorbed and juveniles are free-swimming at approximately 8–10 cm. Clutch sizes are small (10–50 eggs); the male does not feed during incubation. Breeding in home aquaria is extremely rare — virtually all commercially available specimens are produced on licensed farms (primarily in Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia) that maintain broodstock in large earthen ponds. Farm-produced fish are individually microchipped and DNA-certified at the sub-variety level (Super Red, Red Tail Golden, Golden Crossback, Green/Banjar). Breeding attempts at home require a proven male–female pair, enormous tanks (5000+ L), near-wild water conditions, and years of conditioning. Even on farms, successful spawning requires careful pairing; the male may swallow eggs if disturbed.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Scleropages formosus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 90 cm TL), water parameters (pH 6.0–7.0, temperature 24–30°C, soft water), native range (Southeast Asia — Sumatra, Borneo, Malay Peninsula, Indochina), habitat (blackwater peat swamp/flooded forest), diet (surface predator: insects, fish, frogs), and breeding (paternal mouthbrooder, 6–8 week incubation). CITES Appendix I listing noted; trade legal only from licensed farms with microchip/DNA documentation. SeriouslyFish classifies this as a demanding, expert-only species requiring very large tanks.

additional.name: FishBase — Scleropages formosus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max reported 90 cm TL), temperature range (24–30°C), taxonomy (Osteoglossidae, Osteoglossiformes), native range and IUCN Endangered status. CITES Appendix I status confirmed. FishBase lists the species as endangered in the wild; aquaculture captive-bred stock sustains legal trade.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Arowana Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-side care guide. Confirmed large tank requirement (300+ gallon / ~1135 L minimum cited for silver arowana; Asian arowana needs equivalent or larger), carnivore diet, expert care level, jumping risk (tight lid essential), and surface-swimming habit. Noted Asian arowana import restriction in the United States due to CITES Appendix I / Endangered Species Act — sale and ownership of wild-caught and farm-bred specimens is federally prohibited in the USA. Legal in most of Southeast Asia, Europe, and Australia with proper CITES documentation.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Asian Arowana Species Profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist trade publication. Confirmed CITES Appendix I status and farm-chip requirements for legal trade. Noted sub-variety colour forms (Super Red, Red Tail Golden, Golden Crossback, Green/Banjar) with significant price differentials. Described drop-eye syndrome risk from bottom-gazing, paternal mouthbrooding behaviour, and minimum tank footprint requirements for UK keepers. Recommended species-only or carefully chosen large bottom-dweller companions.

additional.name: CITES — Appendix I Listing: Scleropages formosus
additional.notes: Official CITES appendices listing. Scleropages formosus has been listed on CITES Appendix I since 1975, prohibiting commercial international trade in wild-caught specimens. An annotation permits trade in captive-bred specimens from registered aquaculture operations provided each fish is individually microchipped and accompanied by valid CITES export and import permits. USA import remains prohibited under the Endangered Species Act regardless of farm origin. Buyers outside the USA should obtain documentation verifying farm-of-origin, microchip number, and valid CITES permits before purchase.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates eggs and fry buccally for 6–8 weeks. Breeding in home aquaria is essentially not achievable — all commercial stock originates from licensed farms (Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia) with microchip/DNA certification per CITES requirements. Even farm breeding requires large earthen ponds, proven pairs, and years of conditioning.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches ~90 cm and requires 1500 L+ tanks; CITES Appendix I + US Endangered Species Act import ban regardless of farm origin.
